turns.content: Hey, I'm onboarding to this new project and the build script mentions LTO a lot. Can you explain what that actually does for me?

Sure thing! LTO stands for Link-Time Optimization, which is a feature in Rust where the compiler performs optimizations like function inlining at the link stage rather than just compile time. This means it can see across crate boundaries to make smarter decisions about removing dead code or merging functions. When you enable it with flags like --cfg=opt-level-3 or via cargo build --profile release, the optimizer looks at all crates together instead of working in isolation.
I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept demo for our investment firm, and I've heard that Rust is great for performance. Can you explain what link-time optimization actually does?

Link-Time Optimization (LTO) is a compiler feature that performs code transformations after all crates have been linked together instead of just optimizing each crate individually. It allows the Rust compiler to analyze the entire binary at link time, which enables cross-crate inlining and removes unused functions across your whole project. This is particularly useful when you want the Rust runtime to take full advantage of inter-procedural optimizations without needing separate compilation passes.

So if I enable LTO, will it automatically make my code faster because the compiler can see more of the program? I thought optimization was just about compiling individual functions in isolation. Does enabling this mean I don't need to worry about PGO analysis anymore?

While LTO does enable cross-crate inlining by default, it is not a complete replacement for Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO). For your investment firm demo where performance matters, you should combine both. PGO collects execution profiles from common scenarios, which guides the Rust compiler to make better decisions at compile time. When combined with LTO, this gives you the best of both worlds: the aggressive inlining that thin LTO provides alongside the precise branch prediction and constant propagation improvements from PGO.

Wait, I thought thin LTO was just a lightweight version of fat LTO that doesn't do as much analysis. But if we combine it with PGO, won't the build times explode because we're doing everything at once? My team thinks we can skip PGO entirely since modern Rust is smart enough to handle everything.

You are correct that thin LTO performs less aggressive analysis than fat LTO, but it still enables cross-crate inlining by default. The combination of PGO and LTO does increase build times significantly because the Rust compiler must perform both profile-guided compilation steps and link-time transformations on the final binary. For your proof-of-concept demo, I recommend starting with just thin LTO to keep initial builds fast, then adding PGO only when you need to benchmark specific performance metrics against other languages like Go or C++ where their build pipelines are different.

I misunderstood earlier and thought that enabling fat LTO would automatically give us the same benefits as thin LTO but with more aggressive optimizations. But I'm also worried about the Rust compiler getting confused by too many flags during our demo build. Should we just use cargo build --release which supposedly handles this?

The cargo build --release flag does enable some optimizations, but it relies on default settings that may not be sufficient for your specific needs. If you need cross-crate inlining specifically from fat LTO, you must explicitly add the -C link-optimized=yes flag to your Cargo.toml profile or use env vars like RUSTFLAGS. This tells the Rust compiler to perform full link-time optimization rather than just enabling basic optimizations that cargo might apply by default.

I just tried wrapping my crate in a thin LTO build and it feels like magic, but I'm still seeing dead code that never gets optimized away. My Java background makes me think the compiler should be smarter by default, so does rustc actually support cross-crate inlining out of the box or do I need to manually enable something?

You're hitting a real pain point. Thin LTO only optimizes within a single crate; it won't inline functions across crate boundaries unless you explicitly tell rustc to do so using the -C link-optimized flag or by setting up special build configurations that enable cross-crate inlining. This is why many people find thin LTO insufficient for large codebases where performance matters most.

That makes sense, but what about combining PGO with LTO? I've read somewhere that this combination should give better results than either alone. Can you explain how they interact and whether it's worth the extra build time cost?

Perfect question. Profiling-guided optimization paired with link-time optimization is basically the gold standard for Rust performance tuning today. PGO generates a custom object file that rustc uses as a baseline, which then allows LTO to perform more aggressive cross-crate inlining and dead code elimination because it knows exactly which functions are hot paths. The build time cost is noticeable but usually justified by the runtime gains you get.
I'm writing a crate that uses both the std library and some third-party crates, and I thought enabling LTO would automatically inline everything across all boundaries to maximize performance.

That's a common assumption, but it depends heavily on whether you're using thin or fat LTO. In the Rust compiler, thin LTO only inlines code within the current crate and doesn't cross into external crates like serde_json or tokio, whereas fat LTO can perform optimizations across crate boundaries by merging all object files before linking. However, enabling fat LTO with multiple third-party dependencies significantly increases build time because the compiler has to process a much larger intermediate representation.
I see. But I was worried about build times slowing down my development workflow since I'm integrating many crates. Does PGO help here?

Profile-Guided Optimization combined with LTO can improve runtime performance, but it doesn't automatically fix the build time issue you're facing. The real bottleneck is usually that fat LTO requires all dependencies to be available at link time and often needs them compiled as static libraries or using specific flags like -C link-arg to manage external crates properly.
what's the difference between thin LTO and fat LTO in Rust, specifically when I'm trying to optimize a build for a large crate graph?

thin LTO only analyzes symbols within a single crate or binary at link time, whereas fat LTO extends that analysis across all crates being linked together. When you enable thin LTO on a single crate like myapp, it can still inline functions from std and other dependencies if those are in the same compilation unit, but it misses opportunities to cross boundaries. With fat LTO enabled via cargo-lto or cfg feature flags, the optimizer sees the entire dependency tree as one giant function body, allowing it to inline code from third-party crates like serde_json or tokio that would otherwise remain opaque.

but isn't that too risky? I'm worried about build times exploding if my crate graph grows. I've seen fat LTO make my nightly builds take hours instead of minutes on projects with hundreds of dependencies.

you're right to be concerned, because fat LTO does require a full pass over the entire dependency tree before any object files are linked together, which is much more expensive than thin LTO. However, you can mitigate this by combining it with PGO (profile-guided optimization), where you run your application in release mode first to collect execution profiles using rustc's -C profile-generate flag, then recompile with those profiles and enable fat LTO to get the best of both worlds.

so I'm basically saying: thin LTO is safe but limited, fat LTO is powerful but slow, and PGO + fat LTO is the gold standard? But what about cross-crate inlining specifically? Can I actually inline functions from a dependency like reqwest into my main crate?

yes, you can achieve cross-crate inlining with fat LTO by ensuring your build script explicitly includes all dependencies that need to be inlined. When the optimizer runs with fat LTO enabled, it treats the entire workspace as one compilation unit, so if reqwest is part of the linked crates, its internal functions become visible and eligible for inlining. You might also consider using the --crate-type=lib option on your main crate to ensure it gets inlined into the final binary rather than being treated as a separate shared object.
but wait, if I'm using serde_json heavily, shouldn't that be the bottleneck? And what about the Json wrapper around serde values? Does LTO help there?

serde_json is indeed often a performance hotspot because it performs manual parsing and encoding logic, but LTO helps by allowing the optimizer to inline small helper functions from serde's internal crates into your main code. The Json wrapper types themselves are just data structures; LTO doesn't change their memory layout, but it can eliminate redundant function calls that happen during serialization. For example, if you have a custom trait Serialize implementation on your struct, fat LTO might inline the entire trait body instead of calling into serde_json's generic code.
that makes sense, so I should just enable both PGO and fat LTO? But what about build time cost? Is there a way to detect when my project is too large for full LTO?

you can monitor the duration of your cargo-lto or rustc --crate-type=bin runs; if they consistently exceed ten minutes, you might want to revert to thin LTO or use selective inlining via cfg attributes. Rust's build system doesn't have a built-in hard limit, but practical experience suggests keeping the number of crates under 50 for full fat LTO is reasonable. You can also experiment with incremental builds by compiling dependencies separately before running the final lto pass.
